title: Intra-articular Pharmacological Injections for Temporomandibular Joint Osteoarthritis Are Comparable to Placebo
research_question: In adult patients with temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis (TMJ OA), is the intra-articular injection of either corticosteroids (CCS), hyaluronic acid (HA), platelet-rich plasma (PRP), or placebo effective in reducing joint pain?
methods_summary: Systematic review with network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
Search of PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, and gray literature via ClinicalTrials.gov
Two reviewers independently assessed risk of bias using dimensions of quality in RCTs
Network meta-analysis allowing direct and indirect comparisons, including with placebo
dataset: 9 randomized controlled trials involving 316 patients with TMJ OA
key_findings: Intra-articular injections of CCS, HA, or PRP had no significant effect on pain reduction compared with placebo
CCS injection was significantly worse than placebo for short-term pain reduction (MD = 1.73; 95% CI 0.37-3.09)
Most results were not statistically significant, with wide confidence intervals and heterogeneity
Only one study had a large sample (102 patients); the rest were small and underpowered
extracted_insights: Current best evidence from a well-conducted network meta-analysis indicates that intra-articular injections of corticosteroids, hyaluronic acid, or platelet-rich plasma for TMJ OA do not provide superior pain reduction compared to placebo. The findings are limited by small sample sizes, heterogeneity, and unclear risk of bias in included studies, highlighting the need for larger, high-quality RCTs. Clinicians should exercise caution in using these injections for TMJ OA.
